result:
Most of what we do, day to day, is a negotiation with limits.

Consider attention. It behaves less like a spotlight and more like a campfire. You can build a big blaze that lights a wide circle, or a small, hot flame that lets you see one thing in detail. You don’t get both at once. Every time you choose to focus deeply, you are choosing not to know a thousand other things that are happening elsewhere.

This is easy to see in the age of the internet. There has never been a time when so much information was technically available, and so little of it can be meaningfully held in a single mind. Documentation, lectures, code, books, papers—there’s an illusion that if you had enough discipline, you could “catch up,” become well-informed in the full sense of the word.

But depth is made of exclusion. To be truly good at mathematics, or music, or medicine, you must let go of an enormous number of alternate lives you might have had. The cost is real and unpayable in retrospect: when you are an expert cardiologist, you are not also a novelist, a violinist, a historian—at least not at the same level. You bought clarity in one domain with the currency of everything else.

This gives a strange flavor to modern aspiration. We talk about “learning more,” “doing more,” “being more productive,” as if the main enemy is laziness. Often the real enemy is refusal to accept tradeoffs. We want to be widely informed, deeply skilled, consistently present for friends and family, physically healthy, emotionally available, creative—and we want all of this at once, without loss. That is not how finite beings operate.

You can feel the friction in tiny ways:

- Reading ten articles lightly versus sitting with one page until you can explain it from memory.
- Skimming notifications every few minutes versus letting messages pile up while you finish a sentence, a paragraph, a thought.
- Choosing a single problem worth caring about for years versus constantly shopping for a more interesting difficulty.

The first choices feel safer. They maintain the sense that all options are still open—nothing has been given up. But under the surface, a different loss quietly accumulates: the loss of depth, of the kind of understanding that only comes from sustained, exclusive commitment.

The paradox is that commitment looks like narrowing but often becomes an expansion. If you study one subject deeply, other fields start to connect to it. Focus in music teaches you about pattern and structure that spills over into language or math. Focus in programming teaches you about systems and constraints that show up in economics, biology, even relationships. Deep attention in one place sharpens how you see in others.

The danger isn’t specialization; it’s unexamined drift. Going with the default flow of algorithms, recommendations, social expectations—without articulating any deliberate tradeoffs—means you pay the cost of limitation without getting the benefits of chosen focus. You end up scattered, tired, and oddly undernourished, despite constant stimulation.

A practical question, then, is not “How do I fit more in?” but “What am I willing to neglect on purpose?”

That sounds harsh, but it’s clarifying. If you say, for example, “For this season of my life, I am willing to neglect being ‘caught up’ on news, in order to seriously learn X,” you can judge your days in a more honest way. Or, “I am willing to let professional ambitions slow down so that I can be extremely present for this particular relationship, or this particular child, or this particular piece of my own health.”

There is no universal correct answer. The world will happily flood you with urgent-seeming tasks that crowd out anything slow and deep. If you don’t name your priorities, something else—market forces, other people’s goals, the structure of your job or your social media feeds—will do it for you.

And under that sits an even more uncomfortable truth: there isn’t enough time to become the person you could theoretically be in all directions. There is only enough time to become a few things, done properly, with care.

In that light, “wasting time” can be misnamed. If you spend an afternoon walking without headphones, or rereading a single poem, or talking with one friend about one problem, that can look inefficient on a calendar. But in terms of depth—of making some small area of your life less shallow—that might be the most honest use of limitation: you spent your irreplaceable attention on something you truly meant to see.

You can’t escape constraints, but you can choose which ones you honor. That quiet choice, made repeatedly, is a large part of how a life takes shape.
